verses.text: ‘Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said.’ Bildad’s third and final speech is the shortest in the book — only six verses. The friends are running out of arguments. The cycle of three rounds has been wearing them down. Sometimes the loudest theological positions become quieter when their formulas no longer fit the case.

‘Dominion and fear are with him, he maketh peace in his high places.’ Bildad turns to God’s greatness, away from Job’s specifics. The Hebrew oseh shalom — ‘the one who makes peace’ — will resonate forward through Jewish liturgy. Yet here it is offered abstractly, as if reminding Job of God’s majesty might silence his questions. It will not.

‘Is there any number of his armies?’ Bildad pictures God’s heavenly hosts beyond counting. The point is the gap between God and humankind. Verse 4 will press: how then can anyone be righteous before God? The argument is theologically true but pastorally misplaced. Job already knows God is great; what he needs is to know that God is near.

25:4 Eliphaz had said that it is nearly impossible to be innocent or pure (4:17-19; 15:14-16), and then only after suffering (22:21-23). Bildad claimed that only the totally blameless could hope to be pure (8:20).
